{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | Name = Kawahara Shrine | image = File:Kawara-Jinja Kiyosu03.jpg | Location = {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|en|Aichi Prefecture|WD=Q80434|lt=Aichi Prefecture|1=Aichi Prefecture}}{{ill|Kiyosu|en|Kiyosu|WD=Q502407|lt=Kiyosu City|1=Kiyosu}} Aharamiya Higashi | Latitude = 35 | Minutes = 12 | Seconds = 45.63 | Longitude = 136 | Minutes = 51 | Seconds = 27.35 | Enshrined Deity = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|en|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944|lt=Ookuninushi|1=Ōkuninushi}} | Shintai = | Shrine Rank = Former Kyōsha, Hachi-kyūsha | Founded = Unknown | Main Hall = {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|en|Shinmei-zukuri|WD=Q930545|lt=Shinmeizō|1=Shinmei-zukuri}} | Alternate Name = Star Shrine | Fuda etc = | Annual Festival = Second Saturday of October | Rituals = | Map = Japan, Aichi | prefecture= {{ill|Kiyosu|WD=Q502407|1=Kiyosu}} | coordinates= {{Coord|35.212675|N|136.857597|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity= {{ill|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944|1=Ōkuninushi}} | architecture_style= {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|WD=Q930545|1=Shinmei-zukuri}} }}
'''Kawahara Shrine''' (河原神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|en|Aichi Prefecture|WD=Q80434|lt=Aichi Prefecture|1=Aichi Prefecture}} {{ill|Kiyosu|en|Kiyosu|WD=Q502407|lt=Kiyosu City|1=Kiyosu}}.
== History == == Overview == The founding date is unknown<ref name="meikan">{{ill|Aichi Prefecture Shrine Directory|ja|愛知県神社名鑑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aichi Prefecture Shrine Directory|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, pp.227</ref>. The main enshrined deity is {{ill|Ōkuninushi|en|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944|lt=Ookuninushi|1=Ōkuninushi}}, although the ''Honkokuchō'' records it as Juusan-i Kawahara Tenjin<ref name="meikan"/>. In the first year of {{ill|Hōreki|en|Hōreki|WD=Q1194397|lt=Hōreki|1=Hōreki}} ({{ill|1751|en|1751|WD=Q7615|lt=1751|1=1751}}), the published {{ill|Chōshūfushi|ja|張洲府志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōshūfushi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} states, "Kawahara Shrine is located in Aharamura and is now known as Hoshimiya"<ref name="meikan"/>. Similar descriptions are also found in the {{ill|Kiyosu Chronicles|ja|清洲誌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyosu Chronicles|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and in the {{ill|Owari meisho zue|en|Owari meisho zue|WD=Q11465290|lt=Owari Meishodzuukai|1=Owari meisho zue}}<ref name="meikan"/>. It was formerly known as "Hoshinomiya," and the water from the "Shingan Ike" that once gushed from both sides of the haiden was believed to bring relief for eye diseases, attracting many worshippers<ref name="meikan"/>.
=== Modern Period === In {{ill|1907|en|1907|WD=Q2048|lt=1907|1=1907}} (the 40th year of {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}), it was designated as a kyōsha<ref name="meikan"/>. After World War II, it was classified as a jūkkyūsha, but in {{ill|1953|en|1953|WD=Q18585|lt=1953|1=1953}} (the 28th year of {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa|1=Shōwa era}}) it became an hachikyūsha<ref name="meikan"/>. Today, the surrounding area has been urbanized, and only the water tank of Shingan Ike remains. In {{ill|1994|en|1994|WD=Q2067|lt=1994|1=1994}} (the 6th year of {{ill|Heisei era|en|Heisei era|WD=Q234510|lt=Heisei|1=Heisei era}}), a support association was formed together with the nearby {{ill|Yahata Shrine|ja|八幡社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yahata Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 八幡宮|1=Hachiman shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11390939}}, and restoration work was carried out in {{ill|2000|en|2000|WD=Q1985|lt=2000|1=2000}} (the 12th year of {{ill|Heisei era|en|Heisei era|WD=Q234510|lt=Heisei|1=Heisei era}}).
== Buildings == ; {{ill|Haiden (Shinto)|en|Haiden (Shinto)|WD=Q3125745|lt=Haiden|1=Haiden (Shinto)}} : The exact date is unknown, but it is believed to have been constructed from the late Edo period to the Meiji period. The decorative ornamentation features "crane and turtle" on the south side and "rabbit on the moon" on the north side, while the beam caps at the four corners are adorned with carvings of a baku and a lion. There is a record of a major renovation carried out in {{ill|1948|en|1948|WD=Q5165|lt=1948|1=1948}} ({{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa|1=Shōwa era}} 23)<ref name="shiryo">{{ill|Historical Materials Compilation|ja|史料編|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Historical Materials Compilation|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, pp.200–202</ref>.
; {{ill|Honden|en|Honden|WD=Q2044994|lt=Main Hall|1=Honden}} : The main hall, a one-bay shrine in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|en|Shinmei-zukuri|WD=Q930545|lt=Shinmeizō|1=Shinmei-zukuri}} style with cypress bark roofing, was rebuilt in April {{ill|1949|en|1949|WD=Q5188|lt=1949|1=1949}} ({{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa|1=Shōwa era}} 24). It measures 6 shaku by 5 shaku, with a 2-shaku eave on three sides. The supporting handrail is finished with round cypress wood<ref name="shiryo"/>.
; {{ill|Saimon-den|ja|祭文殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saimon-den|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祭文|1=saimon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17991683}} : Built in March {{ill|1911|en|1911|WD=Q2076|lt=1911|1=1911}} (Meiji 44), it is constructed with round cypress wood, featuring a design with a central opening of 8 shaku flanked by sections measuring 5 ken 2 shaku (987.4 cm) on each side. The depth is 1 ken (182 cm)<ref name="shiryo"/>.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Kawara-Jinja_Kiyosu02.jpg|Haiden as seen from the front File:Kawara-Jinja_Kiyosu04.jpg|South side decorative ornamentation of the Haiden File:Kawara-Jinja_Kiyosu06.jpg|Ritual Hall as seen from the front File:Kawara-Jinja_Kiyosu07.jpg|Water tank of Shingan Ike </gallery>
== Access == * [[:en:JR-Central Transport Service Company|JR Central]] {{ill|Jōhoku Line|en|Jōhoku Line|WD=Q6268862|lt=Jōhoku Line|1=Jōhoku Line}} and {{ill|Owari Hoshinomiya Station|ja|尾張星の宮駅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Owari Hoshinomiya Station|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Owari-Hoshinomiya Station|WD=Q7114283}}

== Bibliography == {{commonscat|Kawara Shrine (Kiyosu)}} * 'Aichi Prefecture Shrine Directory', Aichi Prefecture Shrine Office, 1992 * 'Kiyosu Shinkawamachi Historical Materials Compilation 1: Nature, Culture, and Folklore', compiled by the Kiyosu City Shinkawa Town History Committee, Kiyosu City, 2006

'''Kawahara Shrine''' (河原神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|en|Aichi Prefecture|WD=Q80434|lt=Aichi Prefecture|1=Aichi Prefecture}} {{ill|Kiyosu|en|Kiyosu|WD=Q502407|lt=Kiyosu City|1=Kiyosu}}.

== History ==
== Overview ==
The founding date is unknown<ref name="meikan">{{ill|Aichi Prefecture Shrine Directory|ja|愛知県神社名鑑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aichi Prefecture Shrine Directory|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, pp.227</ref>. The main enshrined deity is {{ill|Ōkuninushi|en|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944|lt=Ookuninushi|1=Ōkuninushi}}, although the ''Honkokuchō'' records it as Juusan-i Kawahara Tenjin<ref name="meikan"/>. In the first year of {{ill|Hōreki|en|Hōreki|WD=Q1194397|lt=Hōreki|1=Hōreki}} ({{ill|1751|en|1751|WD=Q7615|lt=1751|1=1751}}), the published {{ill|Chōshūfushi|ja|張洲府志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōshūfushi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} states, "Kawahara Shrine is located in Aharamura and is now known as Hoshimiya"<ref name="meikan"/>. Similar descriptions are also found in the {{ill|Kiyosu Chronicles|ja|清洲誌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyosu Chronicles|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and in the {{ill|Owari meisho zue|en|Owari meisho zue|WD=Q11465290|lt=Owari Meishodzuukai|1=Owari meisho zue}}<ref name="meikan"/>. It was formerly known as "Hoshinomiya," and the water from the "Shingan Ike" that once gushed from both sides of the haiden was believed to bring relief for eye diseases, attracting many worshippers<ref name="meikan"/>.

=== Modern Period ===
In {{ill|1907|en|1907|WD=Q2048|lt=1907|1=1907}} (the 40th year of {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}), it was designated as a kyōsha<ref name="meikan"/>. After World War II, it was classified as a jūkkyūsha, but in {{ill|1953|en|1953|WD=Q18585|lt=1953|1=1953}} (the 28th year of {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa|1=Shōwa era}}) it became an hachikyūsha<ref name="meikan"/>. Today, the surrounding area has been urbanized, and only the water tank of Shingan Ike remains. In {{ill|1994|en|1994|WD=Q2067|lt=1994|1=1994}} (the 6th year of {{ill|Heisei era|en|Heisei era|WD=Q234510|lt=Heisei|1=Heisei era}}), a support association was formed together with the nearby {{ill|Yahata Shrine|ja|八幡社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yahata Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 八幡宮|1=Hachiman shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11390939}}, and restoration work was carried out in {{ill|2000|en|2000|WD=Q1985|lt=2000|1=2000}} (the 12th year of {{ill|Heisei era|en|Heisei era|WD=Q234510|lt=Heisei|1=Heisei era}}).

== Buildings ==
; {{ill|Haiden (Shinto)|en|Haiden (Shinto)|WD=Q3125745|lt=Haiden|1=Haiden (Shinto)}}
: The exact date is unknown, but it is believed to have been constructed from the late Edo period to the Meiji period. The decorative ornamentation features "crane and turtle" on the south side and "rabbit on the moon" on the north side, while the beam caps at the four corners are adorned with carvings of a baku and a lion. There is a record of a major renovation carried out in {{ill|1948|en|1948|WD=Q5165|lt=1948|1=1948}} ({{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa|1=Shōwa era}} 23)<ref name="shiryo">{{ill|Historical Materials Compilation|ja|史料編|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Historical Materials Compilation|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, pp.200–202</ref>.

; {{ill|Honden|en|Honden|WD=Q2044994|lt=Main Hall|1=Honden}}
: The main hall, a one-bay shrine in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|en|Shinmei-zukuri|WD=Q930545|lt=Shinmeizō|1=Shinmei-zukuri}} style with cypress bark roofing, was rebuilt in April {{ill|1949|en|1949|WD=Q5188|lt=1949|1=1949}} ({{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa|1=Shōwa era}} 24). It measures 6 shaku by 5 shaku, with a 2-shaku eave on three sides. The supporting handrail is finished with round cypress wood<ref name="shiryo"/>.

; {{ill|Saimon-den|ja|祭文殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saimon-den|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祭文|1=saimon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17991683}}
: Built in March {{ill|1911|en|1911|WD=Q2076|lt=1911|1=1911}} (Meiji 44), it is constructed with round cypress wood, featuring a design with a central opening of 8 shaku flanked by sections measuring 5 ken 2 shaku (987.4 cm) on each side. The depth is 1 ken (182 cm)<ref name="shiryo"/>.

Today, the surrounding area has been urbanized, and only the water tank of Shingan Ike remains. In {{ill|1994|en|1994|qid=Q2067|lt=1994|1=1994}} (the 6th year of {{ill|Heisei era|en|Heisei era|qid=Q234510|lt=Heisei|1=Heisei era}}), a support association was formed together with the nearby {{ill|Yahata Shrine|ja|八幡社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yahata Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 八幡宮|1=Hachiman shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11390939}}, and restoration work was carried out in {{ill|2000|en|2000|qid=Q1985|lt=2000|1=2000}} (the 12th year of {{ill|Heisei era|en|Heisei era|qid=Q234510|lt=Heisei|1=Heisei era}}).

== Buildings ==
; {{ill|Haiden (Shinto)|en|Haiden (Shinto)|qid=Q3125745|lt=Haiden|1=Haiden (Shinto)}}
: The exact date is unknown, but it is believed to have been constructed from the late Edo period to the Meiji period. The decorative ornamentation features "crane and turtle" on the south side and "rabbit on the moon" on the north side, while the beam caps at the four corners are adorned with carvings of a baku and a lion. There is a record of a major renovation carried out in {{ill|1948|en|1948|qid=Q5165|lt=1948|1=1948}} ({{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|qid=Q327567|lt=Shōwa|1=Shōwa era}} 23)<ref name="shiryo">{{ill|Historical Materials Compilation|ja|史料編|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Historical Materials Compilation|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, pp.200–202</ref>.

; {{ill|Honden|en|Honden|qid=Q2044994|lt=Main Hall|1=Honden}}
: The main hall, a one-bay shrine in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|en|Shinmei-zukuri|qid=Q930545|lt=Shinmeizō|1=Shinmei-zukuri}} style with cypress bark roofing, was rebuilt in April {{ill|1949|en|1949|qid=Q5188|lt=1949|1=1949}} ({{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|qid=Q327567|lt=Shōwa|1=Shōwa era}} 24). It measures 6 shaku by 5 shaku, with a 2-shaku eave on three sides. The supporting handrail is finished with round cypress wood<ref name="shiryo"/>.

; {{ill|Saimon-den|ja|祭文殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saimon-den|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祭文|1=saimon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q17991683}}
: Built in March {{ill|1911|en|1911|qid=Q2076|lt=1911|1=1911}} (Meiji 44), it is constructed with round cypress wood, featuring a design with a central opening of 8 shaku flanked by sections measuring 5 ken 2 shaku (987.4 cm) on each side. The depth is 1 ken (182 cm)<ref name="shiryo"/>.

'''Kawahara Shrine''' (河原神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|en|Aichi Prefecture|qid=Q80434|lt=Aichi Prefecture|1=Aichi Prefecture}} {{ill|Kiyosu|en|Kiyosu|qid=Q502407|lt=Kiyosu City|1=Kiyosu}}.

== History ==
== Overview ==
The founding date is unknown<ref name="meikan">{{ill|Aichi Prefecture Shrine Directory|ja|愛知県神社名鑑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aichi Prefecture Shrine Directory|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, pp.227</ref>. The main enshrined deity is {{ill|Ōkuninushi|en|Ōkuninushi|qid=Q276944|lt=Ookuninushi|1=Ōkuninushi}}, although the ''Honkokuchō'' records it as Juusan-i Kawahara Tenjin<ref name="meikan"/>. In the first year of {{ill|Hōreki|en|Hōreki|qid=Q1194397|lt=Hōreki|1=Hōreki}} ({{ill|1751|en|1751|qid=Q7615|lt=1751|1=1751}}), the published {{ill|Chōshūfushi|ja|張洲府志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōshūfushi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} states, "Kawahara Shrine is located in Aharamura and is now known as Hoshimiya"<ref name="meikan"/>. Similar descriptions are also found in the {{ill|Kiyosu Chronicles|ja|清洲誌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyosu Chronicles|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and in the {{ill|Owari meisho zue|en|Owari meisho zue|qid=Q11465290|lt=Owari Meishodzuukai|1=Owari meisho zue}}<ref name="meikan"/>. It was formerly known as "Hoshinomiya," and the water from the "Shingan Ike" that once gushed from both sides of the haiden was believed to bring relief for eye diseases, attracting many worshippers<ref name="meikan"/>.

=== Modern Period ===
In {{ill|1907|en|1907|qid=Q2048|lt=1907|1=1907}} (the 40th year of {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}), it was designated as a kyōsha<ref name="meikan"/>. After World War II, it was classified as a jūkkyūsha, but in {{ill|1953|en|1953|qid=Q18585|lt=1953|1=1953}} (the 28th year of {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|qid=Q327567|lt=Shōwa|1=Shōwa era}}) it became an hachikyūsha<ref name="meikan"/>. Today, the surrounding area has been urbanized, and only the water tank of Shingan Ike remains. In {{ill|1994|en|1994|qid=Q2067|lt=1994|1=1994}} (the 6th year of {{ill|Heisei era|en|Heisei era|qid=Q234510|lt=Heisei|1=Heisei era}}), a support association was formed together with the nearby {{ill|Yahata Shrine|ja|八幡社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yahata Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 八幡宮|1=Hachiman shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11390939}}, and restoration work was carried out in {{ill|2000|en|2000|qid=Q1985|lt=2000|1=2000}} (the 12th year of {{ill|Heisei era|en|Heisei era|qid=Q234510|lt=Heisei|1=Heisei era}}).

== Buildings ==
; {{ill|Haiden (Shinto)|en|Haiden (Shinto)|qid=Q3125745|lt=Haiden|1=Haiden (Shinto)}}
: The exact date is unknown, but it is believed to have been constructed from the late Edo period to the Meiji period. The decorative ornamentation features "crane and turtle" on the south side and "rabbit on the moon" on the north side, while the beam caps at the four corners are adorned with carvings of a baku and a lion. There is a record of a major renovation carried out in {{ill|1948|en|1948|qid=Q5165|lt=1948|1=1948}} ({{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|qid=Q327567|lt=Shōwa|1=Shōwa era}} 23)<ref name="shiryo">{{ill|Historical Materials Compilation|ja|史料編|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Historical Materials Compilation|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, pp.200–202</ref>.

; {{ill|Honden|en|Honden|qid=Q2044994|lt=Main Hall|1=Honden}}
: The main hall, a one-bay shrine in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|en|Shinmei-zukuri|qid=Q930545|lt=Shinmeizō|1=Shinmei-zukuri}} style with cypress bark roofing, was rebuilt in April {{ill|1949|en|1949|qid=Q5188|lt=1949|1=1949}} ({{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|qid=Q327567|lt=Shōwa|1=Shōwa era}} 24). It measures 6 shaku by 5 shaku, with a 2-shaku eave on three sides. The supporting handrail is finished with round cypress wood<ref name="shiryo"/>.

; {{ill|Saimon-den|ja|祭文殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saimon-den|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祭文|1=saimon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q17991683}}
: Built in March {{ill|1911|en|1911|qid=Q2076|lt=1911|1=1911}} (Meiji 44), it is constructed with round cypress wood, featuring a design with a central opening of 8 shaku flanked by sections measuring 5 ken 2 shaku (987.4 cm) on each side. The depth is 1 ken (182 cm)<ref name="shiryo"/>.
